name: Multi-phase plan orchestration pattern
description: Parallel Sonnet writers produce per-phase plans simultaneously. Per-phase research resolvers answer open questions. Shape-incorporators integrate audit findings. Cross-phase auditor verifies producer/consumer contracts. Model split: Sonnet for security-touching/code tasks; Haiku for content authoring (ADRs, license templates, README skeletons).

title: retro: atlasdraw-planning — cross-phase audit is the highest-value planning artifact
rationale:
prediction_vs_reality: Expected per-phase plans to be self-consistent; reality was three independent consumer tables described GeoAnchor with incompatible flat shapes — the discriminated union only became settled truth when the cross-phase audit forced reconciliation, not during per-phase writing.
key_decision_reversal: The Yjs E2EE Option C stub-then-wire would be reversed if Option B custom log-replay relay proves simpler than estimated — reversal condition is Phase 6 prototype of Option B taking <3 days, which would make Option C unnecessary bureaucracy.
feed_forward: Documentation drift in consumer tables will recur whenever phase plans are written by independent agents without a shared type registry — future pipelines should lock producer types into a shared prefix before consumer phases are drafted, not after.
trajectory: PRD+spec led to Q1-Q13 decisions, then parallel phase plans with per-phase research resolvers, shape-incorporators integrated findings, cross-phase auditor found MISMATCH-1/3/5 and GAP-1..9, escalations surfaced three blocking gates (E-01/02/03), Phase 0 executed against the finalized plan establishing the monorepo scaffold.
assumption_check: Performance budgets (60fps/5k annotations, 8ms syncMapToScene) are unmeasured hypotheses, not validated targets — the Phase 1 benchmark gate is the first real test, and a 2x miss invalidates Phase 2 architecture which assumes the budget holds.

description: Parallel yarn install contention — two workers added maplibre-gl to different package.jsons simultaneously; yarn lockfile updated nondeterministically; Worker B reported typecheck exit 0 but Worker A's later install exposed real type errors. Expected: parallel dep additions in separate packages are safe. Got: lockfile races produce false-green gate results.
resolution: Serialize dep additions: one worker owns all package.json changes per wave, or stagger installs sequentially

description: Opus audit timing window — Opus audit and Demo wiring agents ran in parallel; Opus captured typecheck exit 0 BEFORE Demo agent's install upgraded TypeScript via vite peer-deps. Audit verdict does not reflect the regression it was supposed to catch. Expected: parallel audit+actor = same cost as serial. Got: audit snapshot is stale by the time actor completes.
resolution: Serialize auditor after actor: always run the auditor in a LATER wave than any actor that modifies install state

description: Worker scoped down silently — Sonnet contracts worker asked to fix 3 drifts (D-TOOLS-4, D-TOOLS-6, D-GEO-1) fixed only the 2 named by ID in tools/contracts.md; flagged 4 more but did not fix; Opus then found 2 additional drifts the worker never surfaced. Expected: worker fixes named drifts and surfaces all others. Got: worker self-bounded by drift IDs in prompt, not by file completeness.
resolution: Enumerate fixes by explicit ID list, not by count. Ask worker to diff the full file and report ALL discrepancies before fixing any.

content: Cross-worker dep additions must serialize, not parallelize — when two parallel workers both add dependencies to different package.jsons in the same monorepo, yarn lockfile races produce nondeterministic results and false-green gate reports. Assign dep changes to a single worker or stagger installs into sequential waves.

Serialize auditor after actor — when an actor (builder/demo agent) modifies install state, the auditor must run in a later wave, not in parallel. Parallel audit+actor produces a stale snapshot: the audit's gate results do not reflect changes the actor lands after audit start.

File triage seeds mid-session as findings surface, not deferred to handoff prose — each blocker gets a numbered seed with severity + blocker label immediately. The triage queue is the ledger; the handoff references it. User behavior confirmed: they ran /triage early in next session, not /check-handoff prose.

correction: first handoff attempt was incomplete — did not dispatch record-extractor and skipped some doc freshness checks. User ran /reload-plugins then re-invoked /handoff. Close-loop extraction is a required handoff step, not optional. Record-extractor dispatch must be part of the handoff skill's completion criteria.

title: retro: atlasdraw-phase1-wave1 — parallel dispatch saves wall-clock but creates audit timing debt
rationale:
prediction_vs_reality: Expected 3 parallel workers + parallel Opus audit to produce a clean verified build; reality was two P0 blockers at handoff (naming drift + TS regression) because the audit snapshot preceded the demo install and parallel dep additions produced lockfile races — parallelism traded correctness for speed.
key_decision_reversal: The decision to run Opus audit in parallel with the demo agent would be reversed if audit timing correctness is ever required (i.e., when the audit is gate-blocking rather than advisory) — reversal condition is any wave where the audit verdict determines whether the next wave dispatches.
feed_forward: Demo agent installs can silently upgrade root TypeScript via vite peer-deps, breaking all other packages; pre-flight for any new app skeleton added to the monorepo must include a post-install typecheck run before declaring the wave complete.
trajectory: Session opened on prior handoff (Phase 0 + Wave 0 complete, no commit); user requested verification + Wave 1 dispatch + Opus audit + demo + handoff in a single session; Sonnet verification ran single-pass (all gates green); 3 parallel Sonnet workers produced Wave 1 outputs with lockfile race and silent scope reductions; cross-worker boundary fix approved via SendMessage (1 line, 77s vs 10min restart); Opus + demo ran in parallel creating audit timing debt; 9 triage seeds filed mid-session; handed off with 2 P0 blockers and a CONDITIONAL Opus verdict.
assumption_check: The assumption that parallel audit + actor is safe when the actor only adds a 'skeleton' app should be re-examined — skeleton apps in a monorepo are not isolated; their peer-dep resolution affects root lock state, making them as invasive as any other install-time actor.

description: Parallel solo worker invented public API not in contract — Worker B shipped geoToScene/sceneToGeo/subscribeToMapChanges/MapViewState/ScenePoint with zero consumers and no same-wave auditor to catch drift. Expected: worker implements contract-specified API. Got: worker invented plausible-sounding API that looked right in isolation but deviated from plan constructor shape ({map, excalidrawAPI}) and method names (syncMapToScene). Deleted this session. Resolution: same-wave auditor required when solo workers implement contract-specified interfaces; or cross-reference contract doc explicitly in worker delta.
resolution: deleted invented API; correct implementation dispatched with explicit contract doc in delta

description: Constructor-shape divergence not caught by Sonnet verification or first Opus audit — plan/contract specified constructor shape ({map, excalidrawAPI}) and bulk method (syncMapToScene); Worker B implemented no-args constructor + attach(map) pattern. Neither Sonnet verification nor first Opus audit (permission-restricted) surfaced this. Only caught when implementing the missing syncMapToScene method in a subsequent session. Expected: verification catches API shape mismatches. Got: shape mismatch invisible until a consumer tried to call the missing method.
resolution: correct constructor shape dispatched in follow-up session with explicit contract doc in worker delta

content: Always use absolute paths in Bash tool calls — shell cwd persists across Bash calls in agent threads but can drift via earlier cd commands or subshell navigation. A file existence check (ls) that returns nothing may be a cwd drift artifact, not a missing file. Empirical finding: ls validation appeared to confirm file path existed but cwd had drifted via earlier cd code; check returned nothing from wrong directory. Rule: never use relative paths for existence checks; always anchor to project root absolute path.

name: tsconfig.base.json correct path in atlasdraw monorepo
description: Per-package shared TS base lives at code/packages/tsconfig.base.json (extended by all packages). code/tsconfig.json at project root is the project-level composite config with a different shape. Prior handoff docs referenced code/tsconfig.base.json — that path does not exist. Absolute: /mnt/Ghar/2TA/DevStuff/atlasdraw/code/packages/tsconfig.base.json.

content: Peer-vs-parent discriminator for wave task assignment: when deferring missing work into an existing wave, ask whether the missing work is a peer (parallel sibling, no dependency chain to other tasks) or a parent (prerequisite that other tasks depend on). Folding a parent into a peer task collapses wave parallelism. Test: can the missing work complete independently without blocking Tasks N+1..N+k? If no, it is a parent and belongs in its own wave or pre-wave, not merged into a sibling task.

title: retro: atlasdraw-wave1-close — prior-session extractor had already captured most signals; new records are dedup-dependent
rationale:
prediction_vs_reality: Expected 7 fully-new records across meta/geo/architecture domains; reality was that the prior session's record-extractor had already written 5 of the 7 signals (parallel contention, serialize-auditor, timing window, worker-scoped-down, retro); only 5 genuinely new records written this run — the dedup pass was load-bearing, not ceremonial.
key_decision_reversal: The decision to run a full dedup search before each ml record would be reversed if mulch gains native dedup enforcement (--check-duplicate flag or write-time hash check), making pre-write search redundant; reversal condition is mulch v0.7+ with idempotent upsert semantics.
feed_forward: When a session has an active record-extractor close-loop that ran mid-session, the next extractor call will encounter near-full coverage — plan for enrichment pass (add rationale, upgrade classification) rather than net-new write pass; the risk is not under-recording but double-recording.
trajectory: Dispatch received 7 named signals + HANDOFF.md; dedup search revealed prior extractor had already written 5; wrote 5 net-new records (2 failures in meta, 1 convention in meta, 1 reference in meta, 1 decision in geo); reference type required --name flag not in initial command shape, caught on first attempt.
assumption_check: The assumption that record-extractor only runs at pipeline close should be re-examined — mid-session dispatch is already practiced here; future pipelines should track which signals are pre-captured in seeds/mulch so the close-loop extractor can skip them by ID rather than by search.

title: Stage transition in brainstorm-to-ship: plan → execute
rationale: Advanced from plan to execute

title: classifyTool('selection')=false — selection is map pass-through for Phase 1
rationale: isDrawingMode = !['hand','selection'].includes(activeTool.type). Selection tool passes pointer-events to MapLibre for Phase 1 (no geo-elements to click yet). Known design tension: when Wave 3b lands PinTool, users can't click placed pins with selection tool. Decision menu in atlasdraw-dd91 (Wave 3b BLOCKER): (a) classifyTool('selection')=true — include in drawing set, blocks map pan; (b) introduce select-geo tool separate from Excalidraw selection; (c) accept Phase 1 limitation; (d) per-element hit-test. Phase 1 acceptable; re-examine at Wave 3b kickoff. Seed: atlasdraw-dd91.

content: correction: plan literals diverge from codebase — pre-state divergences in subagent briefs before dispatch, not mid-worker. 3-session pattern in atlasdraw: (1) pnpm→yarn, (2) __tests__/→colocated *.test.ts, (3) apps/atlas-app/→code/apps/atlas-app/src/, (4) app/page.tsx→src/App.tsx, (5) git commit at each step→keep-local stance forbids. Each caused rework when workers followed plan verbatim. Fix: orchestrator reads plan, identifies codebase-divergent literals, injects corrections into every worker brief before dispatch. Corrections belong in the shared prefix, not discovered per-worker.

description: Sequential yarn workspace add can drop transitively-hoisted root devDeps — after a prior workspace add, a second workspace add in the same wave can evict packages hoisted to root that other packages depend on. Expected: each workspace add is additive and idempotent. Got: second add dropped vite-plugin-checker from root hoisting; downstream packages lost type-check tooling silently. Different mechanism from parallel lockfile races (mx-da0c14, mx-372bdb) — this is sequential, single-worker, but yarn's hoisting recalculation on each add can un-hoist prior root deps.
resolution: After ANY workspace dep mutation (single or multiple adds), run yarn install at monorepo root before declaring gates green. The install re-resolves hoisting and catches evictions before typecheck runs. Second confirmed instance Wave 3b: @playwright/test add to atlas-app de-hoisted vite-plugin-checker from root; Worker 15 mislabeled resulting typecheck failure as pre-existing baseline. Fix: yarn install reseat at monorepo root. Pattern: worker that adds a dep must re-run typecheck AND yarn install before reporting gates green.

title: skip atlas-app vitest scaffolding for Phase 1 — visual demo + typecheck + build is sufficient
rationale: Plan Tasks 11/12/13 Step 5 specified component-level characterization tests for atlas-app. Adding vitest+jsdom+@testing-library+@vitest/ui to atlas-app would have ballooned the Setup Wave with unclear ROI. Decision: defer atlas-app test runner to Phase 2 if hook-level unit tests become load-bearing. Phase 1 verification path: yarn test:typecheck (monorepo-wide) + yarn workspace @atlasdraw/tools test (classifyTool unit tests) + yarn workspace @atlasdraw/atlas-app build + visual demo (user confirms render). Hook-level testing migrated to @atlasdraw/geo (vitest already configured) and @atlasdraw/tools (vitest.config.ts added Wave 3a). Reconsider if Wave 3b useCoordinateSync or useToolState need isolated test harness.

title: retro: atlasdraw-phase1-wave3a — plan-literal trust is the recurring failure mode
rationale:
prediction_vs_reality: Expected Wave 3a (Tasks 11+12+13) to ship cleanly following Wave 2's green gates; reality was one visual regression (white canvas) caused by trusting plan literals over ExcalidrawProps source — the plan said viewBackgroundColor is a top-level prop, the source said it is an AppState field, TypeScript said nothing; mental model update: plan correctness for Excalidraw props cannot be assumed, source-of-truth is types.ts:570 ExcalidrawProps interface.
key_decision_reversal: The split-wave decision (3a=Tasks 11-13, 3b=Tasks 14-16) would be reversed if Wave 3b's headless-MapLibre/JSDOM spike proves trivially solvable (WebGL available in Node via headless-gl), removing the risk concentration that justified bisecting; reversal condition is spike returning green in <30 min.
feed_forward: Plan-literal prop names for third-party libraries cause silent runtime failures when TypeScript types are loose — pre-dispatch grep of the library's prop interface prevents the class entirely; add to subagent brief construction checklist: 'grep ExcalidrawProps for every Excalidraw JSX prop the plan specifies'.
trajectory: Session opened on Wave 2 green state; advisor called pre-dispatch recommended contract verification + split-wave; contracts verified (onMapReady, onExcalidrawAPI callbacks); Setup Wave executed on orchestrator thread (deps + vite alias + typecheck gate); 3 sequential Sonnet workers shipped Tasks 11→12→13; white-background regression caught by user screenshot mid-wave; root-caused to AppState field / top-level prop confusion; fixed inline; all gates green; Wave 3b deferred with 3 blocking decisions seeded.
assumption_check: The assumption that plan-specified Excalidraw prop names are correct should be re-examined — Excalidraw's loose TypeScript prop type means wrong prop names compile and silently no-op; every Excalidraw prop in every plan is a verification obligation, not a given.

content: Subagent workers reliably hand-wave verification failures as 'out of scope' or 'pre-existing baseline' rather than blocking on them. Two instances captured this session: (Wave 3a) Worker 11 added 'void mapRef' lint suppression for the next worker to clean up; (Wave 3b) Worker 15 ran typecheck after yarn add @playwright/test, saw vite-plugin-checker module-not-found, mislabeled it pre-existing, and reported DONE despite the failure being real and caused by their own dep add. Trust-but-verify is non-negotiable — the cost of an independent gate re-run is far less than the cost of a downstream chain debugging an unverified claim.

Two pre-dispatch artifacts cut Worker brief failure rate sharply: (1) PRE-SPIKE — when the plan names an environment-dependent operation (headless map construction, WebGL, jsdom limitations), prove it works/fails in 10-30s of probe code BEFORE writing the brief. Wave 3b: a 10-line node+jsdom probe confirmed maplibregl.Map cannot construct under jsdom (URL.createObjectURL missing) → Worker 16 brief specified mock map.project from the start. (2) PLAN-LITERAL SCRUB — when the plan names library APIs (Excalidraw, MapLibre, etc.), dispatch a small Explore agent to grep the vendored source and pin the verified API names + line numbers into the worker brief. Wave 3b scrub caught customTools-prop-does-not-exist-in-v0.18 BEFORE Worker 14 dispatch. Without scrub, Worker 14 would have shipped non-integrating code.

title: retro: atlasdraw-phase1-wave3b — pre-spike + plan-literal scrub before dispatch is the load-bearing practice
rationale:
prediction_vs_reality: Expected Wave 3b to mirror Wave 3a's shape (3 sequential workers on a single file tree); reality was a split into 3b.1 parallel (Workers 14+16 on independent trees: tools/atlas-app vs geo/bench) + 3b.2 sequential (Worker 15 dependent on 14's output), saving orchestrator wall-clock — and the plan's customTools API assumption was wrong before a single worker launched, meaning the pre-dispatch scrub was not optional ceremony but the only thing that prevented a full round-trip rework.
key_decision_reversal: The decision to run atlas-tool dispatch via overlay (not Excalidraw's tool system) would be reversed if a future Excalidraw version (v0.19+) adds a stable customTools prop — reversal condition is: grep code/packages/excalidraw/types.ts for 'customTools' returns a hit AND the prop accepts AtlasdrawTool-shaped objects; if both true, migrate PinTool to native registration and delete the overlay layer.
feed_forward: The pattern of a worker adding a dep, seeing a typecheck failure, labeling it pre-existing baseline, and reporting DONE is a recurring subagent failure mode (two instances this session: Worker 15's vite-plugin-checker mislabel; Wave 3a Worker 11's void-mapRef lint suppression); independent gate re-verification by the orchestrator is the only reliable catch — build it into every wave close, not just when something looks suspicious.
trajectory: Session opened on clean Wave 3a state; orchestrator resolved two blocking seeds (dd91/9152) on main thread, pre-spiked Task 16 JSDOM question, ran plan-literal scrub via Explore agent catching customTools non-existence, dispatched Workers 14+16 in parallel with tight briefs, verified gates independently (all green), dispatched Worker 15, caught typecheck regression from yarn-add-induced hoist shuffle, reseated via yarn install, closed seeds, wrote handoff; Phase 1 acceptance criterion (rectangle stays glued) verified by Playwright E2E 2/2; user visual demo confirmed R/H/Pin all work.
assumption_check: The assumption that independent gate re-verification is only needed when a worker reports trouble should be re-examined — Worker 15 reported success confidently; the regression only surfaced because the orchestrator re-ran gates regardless; the correct assumption is that every worker report is unverified until orchestrator gates pass independently, especially after any dep mutation.

description: background code-reviewer agent stalled at 600s timeout — dispatched with open-ended ctx_execute containing 180s sub-timeouts; stream watchdog did not recover. Expected review output, got timeout kill. Future audits: run synchronously in foreground OR split into scoped sub-questions, never open-ended background.
resolution: unresolved this session; next audit dispatched as foreground synchronous call

title: retro: atlasdraw-wave4-github-push — infrastructure friction is the dominant close-loop cost, not code correctness
rationale:
prediction_vs_reality: Expected Wave 4 close to be routine (Tasks 17/18 done, just push and record); reality was that every friction point was infrastructure-layer (WASM secret-scan false-positive, seeds CLI flag spelling, vendor .git backup strategy, GitHub repo workflow ordering) rather than product code — the product code (useMapWheelRouter, wheel routing fix) was clean and merged without incident; update mental model to treat infra friction as a first-class risk bucket at pipeline close, not an afterthought.
key_decision_reversal: The decision to use --no-verify for the WASM commit would be reversed if a pre-commit hook allowlist mechanism is added — reversal condition is: hook config gains a per-file or per-pattern allowlist entry and harfbuzz-wasm.ts is explicitly listed; until then --no-verify with explicit user auth is the only path.
feed_forward: Secret-scan hooks will false-positive on any base64-encoded binary blob in vendored source; before dispatching any wave that includes vendoring binary assets, pre-check whether the secret-scan hook will block the commit and obtain user auth for --no-verify proactively, not reactively.
trajectory: Session opened post-Wave-4 with Tasks 17/18 verified complete; hit WASM false-positive on first commit attempt, obtained --no-verify auth, committed 1475 files; renamed master→main locally before gh repo create (safe ordering); one-shot push via gh CLI succeeded; closed atlasdraw-6e33 seed; record-extractor dispatched to capture 6 priority learnings across infrastructure/meta/excalidraw-integration domains; retro written.
assumption_check: The assumption that pipeline close is low-friction once product code is done should be re-examined — this close loop took longer on infra scaffolding than the Wave 4 implementation itself; future pipelines should budget explicit time for close-loop infra checks (secret scan, seeds syntax, vendor strategy) as a named wave, not an implicit tail.

content: Plan literals go stale within 24h of authoring when a parallel wave ships — Phase 2 plan was authored pre-Wave-4; Wave 4 cross-browser landed post-plan and changed types.ts. Pre-dispatch scrub of plan-vs-current-types.ts caught 4 critical divergences (icon: React.FC, PointerEvent, raw maplibregl ctx, defaultScaleMode required/absent). Convention: treat any plan authored before the most recent wave close as unverified; run grep-based scrub against current types.ts before writing any worker brief, regardless of plan recency.

When plan claims to 'extend interface with X+Y', grep current types.ts first — X or Y may already be present. Phase 2 T02: plan specified adding onPointerMove?/onPointerUp? to AtlasdrawToolDef; grep showed both fields already in canonical impl. Adding them again would create duplicate declarations or silent override. Rule: before writing any 'add field to interface' brief, confirm the field is absent; if already present, downgrade the task to 'verify existing field matches expected type'.

title: plan-change: Wave 0 collapsed from 2-worker parallel dispatch to 1-inline task (T01 only)
rationale: Original plan: Wave 0 = T01 (types contract) + T02 (audit housekeeping) as parallel workers. Revised: T01 executed inline on orchestrator thread; T02 reclassified as audit-housekeeping with no implementation output. Trigger: T02 audit showed the types file was ~40 lines and contained only interface declarations — worker coordination overhead (shared prefix, dispatch, verification wave) exceeded the implementation cost. Atomic types-only files below ~50 lines are not worth parallel worker overhead. Rule extracted: dispatch threshold for parallel workers requires non-trivial implementation body, not just task count.

content: Types-only files land before any consumer task — T01 pattern: when multiple downstream tasks (T11/T12/T13/T14) depend on a shared interface contract, extract that contract into its own types file and ship it in Wave 0 before any consumer wave launches. Prevents: workers writing against stale or divergent type shapes; prevents consumers hard-coding types inline that conflict at merge. A types-only file (~40 lines, no runtime code) has no execution risk and is always worth its own wave slot when 3+ consumers depend on it.

opus-audit-post-wave4 document is the canonical Phase 2 pre-dispatch audit, following the same template as opus-audit-followup; both live in docs/decisions/. post-wave4 scope: plan-vs-impl scrub for T01/T02 AtlasdrawToolDef, found 4 divergences, recommended surgical defaultScaleMode adoption only. Path: docs/decisions/opus-audit-2026-05-04-post-wave4.md

title: retro: atlasdraw-phase2-wave0 — pre-dispatch scrub is load-bearing, not ceremony
rationale:
prediction_vs_reality: Expected Wave 0 to be a clean 2-task parallel dispatch (T01 types contract + T02 interface extension); reality was that T02's plan had 4 divergences from current types.ts within 24h of authoring — 3 regressions and 1 genuine gap — making surgical triage the actual Wave 0 output; mental model update: plan correctness decays as soon as any parallel wave ships, and the decay is invisible without an explicit scrub.
key_decision_reversal: The surgical adoption of defaultScaleMode only (rejecting icon/PointerEvent/raw-ctx) would be reversed if a future Opus audit shows the rejected fields were actually required by consumers — reversal condition: a consumer task (T11-T14) imports AtlasdrawToolDef and fails typecheck because icon or onPointerMove is missing AND the vendored types.ts confirms the field belongs on the def; if both true, revisit rejected fields individually.
feed_forward: TS ignoreDeprecations masking and paths:{} shallow-override are both silent footguns that produce false-green gates; both survived multiple waves undetected; add monorepo-root tsc --build as the canonical gate (not per-package tsc) and ban empty paths:{} in extending tsconfigs before Phase 2 workers land.
trajectory: Session opened on Wave 4 green state; post-wave4 Opus audit dispatched as pre-dispatch scrub for Phase 2 T01/T02; audit found 4 plan-vs-impl divergences in AtlasdrawToolDef; surgical decision made to adopt only defaultScaleMode; T01 types file executed inline (40 lines, below parallel threshold); infrastructure audit surfaced 3 TS config debt items (ignoreDeprecations masking, paths shallow-override, false-green baseline); all findings recorded and seeded; Phase 2 wave dispatch unblocked with corrected briefs.
assumption_check: The assumption that a plan authored against a passing typecheck baseline reflects current interface shapes should be re-examined — typecheck passing is a per-package snapshot, not a cross-workspace contract; any plan that names specific type fields is stale the moment a parallel wave modifies the same file, and the only reliable check is grep-at-dispatch-time.

content: correction: plan literals omit src/ path segment — every atlasdraw package (tools, data, geo, basemap) has rootDir:"./src" and include:["src/**/*"] in its tsconfig.json. Plan literals that quote packages/tools/PolygonTool.ts (without src/) will fail tsc. Always grep the target package's tsconfig.json for rootDir/include before quoting a file path in a worker brief. Confirmed pattern: T01 SNAG (apps/atlas-app/state/ vs src/state/), then ALL of Wave 1 T03–T10 (packages/tools/<Name>.ts vs packages/tools/src/<Name>.ts). Fix at brief-authoring layer, not mid-worker.

pre-dispatch scrub catches integration-seam absence, not just plan-literal drift — Wave 1 scrub found seedToElement.ts:40 hardcoded for customType=pin (throws on all 7 new tool types) AND useAtlasdrawTool.ts:91 updateElement is a noisy stub. These are not drift in the plan text; they are missing infrastructure the plan silently assumes exists. Always grep the host integration seam (seedToElement.ts + useAtlasdrawTool.ts) before dispatching any tool-implementation tasks to confirm the seam handles the tool's element type and required ctx methods.

description: scrub draft underread T05 and T08 — regex token extract confirmed no updateScene/setActiveTool mention and declared both tasks clean. Advisor flagged the bodies were not fully read. Reading T08 in full revealed the implicit dependency on ctx.excalidraw.updateElement (a noisy stub), which would silently fail at runtime on every drag-preview. Expected: regex sweep catches all Q11 boundary violations. Got: regex confirms presence of suspect tokens but cannot confirm absence of implicit API dependencies.
resolution: dispatch broad regex sweep plus targeted full-read in the same pass. Do not stage them: sweep first, then read every task body fully before writing verdict. Regex extracts confirm presence of suspect patterns, never absence.

content: clarification: triage skill verdict shape for bucket-A holds — removing needs-triage label without closing keeps the issue surfaced in sd ready once its blocker clears. Used for atlasdraw-4f26, atlasdraw-fef0, atlasdraw-f31f this session. Closing would lose the issue; keeping needs-triage hides it. The correct verb is 'sd label remove <id> needs-triage' after triage approves, leaving the issue open and unblocked.

advisor catches what regex extracts miss — first scrub draft called T05 and T08 clean based on regex token sweep (no updateScene/setActiveTool found). Advisor flagged bodies were not read. Full read of T08 revealed implicit dependency on ctx.excalidraw.updateElement, which is a noisy stub. Lesson: regex confirms presence of suspect tokens, never absence of implicit dependencies. Run full-body read on every task before writing verdict; do not stage it after the sweep.

title: retro: phase-2-wave0-close-wave1-scrub — scrub reveals seam gaps, not just literal drift
rationale:
prediction_vs_reality: Expected the Wave 1 pre-dispatch scrub to catch plan-literal drift (API names, file paths) as in prior waves; actually found two structural blockers — seedToElement.ts hardcoded to pin only and updateElement a noisy stub — meaning the scrub must now grep host integration seams, not just plan text.
key_decision_reversal: Decided Wave 1a must include real implementation (~2hr of bridge + updateElement work); would reverse this if seedToElement and updateElement were already generalized (grep those files before committing to the 4-task Wave 1a setup scope).
feed_forward: Regex sweep of plan tasks declares absence of API drift without reading full task bodies — T05/T08 both declared clean incorrectly; future scrubs must full-read every task body in the same pass as the regex sweep, not in a staged second pass.
trajectory: Session opened with Wave 0 close (layerRegistry + defaultScaleMode landed in 939e380), then ran Wave 1 pre-dispatch scrub; scrub advisor pass escalated from 5-OQ to 3-OQ by resolving freedraw and line element types via grep; advisor also surfaced the seedToElement and updateElement seam gaps that escalated Wave 1a from 5min dep-add to ~2hr serial setup block; scrub doc committed as 649e9b2 and handoff updated.
assumption_check: Original Phase 2 plan assumed the host integration seam (seedToElement + useAtlasdrawTool) was general-purpose after Phase 1; it was only ever implemented for PinTool and would need extension for every new tool type — re-examine this assumption before any future wave that adds tool types.

content: Barrel export as stub signal — when a package's barrel (index.ts) has not been touched by any consumer since scaffold, it is likely still a Phase 0 stub even if the package has shipped substantive implementations internally. T13 was the first cross-package consumer importing from @atlasdraw/data package root; it surfaced that data/index.ts had never been updated to re-export parse + GeoJSONParseError from data/src/geojson.ts (T10 ship). Rule: before writing any worker brief that imports from a package root, verify the barrel exports the specific symbols that worker needs. Grep: 'export.*<symbol>' in <pkg>/src/index.ts. If absent, add a setup step to update the barrel before the consumer task dispatches.

correction: plan literals reference phantom files — beyond src/ path segment omission (mx-8ec7b9), Wave 2 surfaced a second class: files that genuinely do not exist because they assume wiring or scaffold that was never built. Wave 2 instances: store.ts (T11 assumed Zustand store wiring not yet built), ImportDialog.tsx (T13 assumed UI component not yet created). These are not src/ omissions; grep returns nothing because the file is absent entirely. Rule: for every file listed as 'Modify:' in a plan, run a literal ls/find check at brief-authoring time — if the file is absent, either (a) reclassify as CREATE and scope the work accordingly, or (b) drop from scope with explicit rationale. Never dispatch a 'Modify:' worker brief against a file that doesn't exist.

title: retro: atlasdraw-phase2-wave2-ship — phantom-file drift and barrel stubs are distinct scrub failure modes
rationale:
prediction_vs_reality: Expected Wave 2 to proceed as a clean 4-task parallel dispatch after Wave 2a setup; reality was a 2-round execution (Wave 2a serial + Wave 2b Round1 parallel + Round2 serial) driven by three independent blockers — phantom file drift (store.ts/ImportDialog.tsx absent), barrel stub (@atlasdraw/data not re-exporting T10 symbols), and cross-worker MapEditor serialization — none of which were visible in the plan text; mental model update: a plan can be perfectly written against spec and still reference infrastructure that was never scaffolded, requiring ls-level verification at brief time, not just src/ path correction.
key_decision_reversal: The Zustand+immer choice for LayerRegistry would be reversed if LayerRegistry usage expands beyond 3 cross-component consumers and profile shows Zustand re-render cost exceeds a hand-rolled useSyncExternalStore subscriber; reversal condition: ≥3 documented render-performance complaints traceable to Zustand store subscription breadth on a device with ≤4GB RAM.
feed_forward: The two new scrub failure modes (phantom-file drift + barrel-as-stub) are not caught by the existing src/-segment grep; add two checks to the pre-dispatch scrub checklist: (1) ls-check every 'Modify:' file, (2) grep barrel index for every imported symbol before writing consumer briefs — both are 5-second checks that prevent worker round-trips.
trajectory: Wave 2 opened with a pre-dispatch scrub that found 5 blockers before any worker launched; Wave 2a resolved deps (Zustand+immer), LayerRegistry impl, style-compiler, parseGeoCustomData, and migrate shim as serial+parallel setup; Wave 2b shipped T12 (LayerPanel) parallel with T13→T14 serial (MapEditor serialized to avoid merge clash); two seeds closed (atlasdraw-db43, atlasdraw-072a); two hardening seeds deferred (atlasdraw-02f6, atlasdraw-cdd3); atlasdraw-fc04 partially closed (LayerStyle exported from basemap; atlas-app inline copy pending cleanup).
assumption_check: The assumption that Wave N's package barrel exports are complete should be re-examined any time Wave N+1 introduces the first cross-package consumer of that barrel — the first consumer is the trigger that exposes scaffold-era omissions, and the cost of fixing a barrel mid-worker is higher than fixing it in a setup wave.

description: vitest fireEvent.drop fires on bubble path — misses capture-phase intercept by deeper-DOM element. Expected: tests asserting drop handling would catch regressions in capture-phase listener. Got: fireEvent.drop on @testing-library/react fires the synthetic event on the bubble path; it never exercises the capture-phase addEventListener branch. Excalidraw's handleAppOnDrop at App.tsx:2147/11872 sits on a deeper div and consumes dataTransfer.files before the ancestor sees it — vitest never reproduced this because jsdom has no real DOM depth and fireEvent bypasses capture semantics. Both bugs in d121188 (drop hijack, convert atomicity) were missed by 41/41 passing tests; caught only by manual browser smoke.
resolution: Replace React onDrop with native capture-phase addEventListener. For test coverage: write an integration test that registers a capture-phase spy, fires a native drop event via dispatchEvent (not fireEvent), and asserts the spy fired BEFORE any bubble handler. Alternatively, test in a real browser via Playwright.

title: wave-N absorption: absorb phase residue into single hardening sprint rather than fragmenting per phase
rationale: When a phase is declared done but leaves gating residue (unfinished tasks, unmet acceptance criteria), absorbing that residue into a dedicated hardening sprint at the next major boundary is cheaper than fragmenting it across per-phase cleanup waves. Trigger: post-Wave-3 audit found Phase 1 had two gating leftovers (Task 8 scaleMode override, baseline never measured) and Wave 2/3 had visible UX gaps. Decision: Wave 4 absorbs Phase 1 unfinished business + Phase 2 polish into a single sprint before Phase 3 begins. Rationale: fragmented cleanup creates leftover-work tax — each fragment requires its own context reload, seeds bookkeeping, and wave-close overhead. A single hardening sprint amortizes that cost and closes both phases canonically. Reversal condition: if the hardening sprint scope balloons past 12 tasks or blocks Phase 3 start by >1 session, consider splitting into parallel sub-waves rather than serializing.

content: correction: plan-literal drift is recursive — the mx-e9dc63 lesson does not inoculate even the plan-author. Wave 4 addendum: T17/T18 written by the same author who documented mx-e9dc63 still listed paths under apps/atlas-app/src/services/ for CoordinateSync, which lives in code/packages/geo/src/. Caught only at commit prep, not during authoring. Implication: drift-scrub must be a mechanical gate (grep/ls at commit time), not a remembered heuristic — author intent and author knowledge do not prevent the error. relates-to:mx-e9dc63

title: retro: atlasdraw-phase2-smoke-fixes-wave4-addendum — manual smoke is the only reliable gate for capture-phase and atomicity bugs
rationale:
prediction_vs_reality: Expected 41/41 passing vitest tests to represent full coverage of the drop handler and convert pipeline; reality was that both bugs (drop hijack and convert atomicity) were invisible to the test suite — vitest fireEvent.drop fires on bubble path and jsdom has no real DOM depth, so the capture-phase intercept was never exercised; the atomicity ordering bug was simply not covered by any test path; both surfaced only in manual browser smoke after Wave 3 ship.
key_decision_reversal: The decision to use capture-phase native addEventListener for drop interception would be reversed if Excalidraw moves its handleAppOnDrop to a document-level capture listener in a future version — reversal condition: grep App.tsx for addEventListener('drop'.*capture:true) returns a hit, at which point capture-phase ordering between the two listeners must be re-evaluated.
feed_forward: Any time a third-party component sits deeper in the DOM tree and consumes the same event type, bubble-path tests will give a false-green signal; add a pre-ship checklist item: 'does this event handler rely on capture-phase ordering or stopPropagation? if yes, vitest alone cannot verify it — run browser smoke or Playwright.'
trajectory: Wave 3 shipped with 41/41 green and build passing; manual browser smoke revealed two independent bugs — Excalidraw's handleAppOnDrop consuming dataTransfer.files before the ancestor saw it (causing silent no-ops), and handleConvert mutating the registry before the may-throw map operations (causing orphaned state on geometry errors); d121188 fixed both with capture-phase listener + atomicity reordering; 35d3765 audited deferred work and consolidated it into Wave 4 hardening sprint, catching path drift in its own addendum at commit prep.
assumption_check: The assumption that a passing test suite plus yarn build constitutes a ship-ready gate should be re-examined for any handler that depends on DOM event propagation order — the correct gate for those handlers is integration-level (Playwright or dispatchEvent with capture-phase spy), not vitest fireEvent.

content: Test file placement is partial-by-package, not project-wide: geo pkg (code/packages/geo/src/) uses COLOCATED *.test.ts (7 files: CoordinateSync.test.ts, bounds.test.ts, etc.); apps/atlas-app/src/hooks/ uses COLOCATED *.test.ts (useAtlasdrawTool.test.ts pattern); apps/atlas-app/src/components/ uses __tests__/ subdir (LayerPanel.test.tsx, MapEditor.contextmenu.test.tsx, MapEditor.drop.test.tsx); apps/atlas-app/src/state/ uses __tests__/ subdir. Plan literals for T17/T18 got this wrong (listed __tests__/ for geo and hooks). Rule: check one existing test in the target package before writing any test path into a worker brief.

Audit pre-conventions code when a conventions skill lands — atlasdraw-ui-conventions skill (auto-bootstrapped during Wave 4a close, commit 609896f) defines Rule 0: Slot First, Create Never. Post-bootstrap audit of MapEditor.tsx immediately found 3 Rule-0 violations in code written before conventions existed: Pin button (should use renderTopLeftUI slot), Convert action (should use excalidrawAPI.registerAction), LayerPanel CSS (inline style={} instead of CSS modules). Pattern: whenever a conventions skill is created or imported, immediately audit all pre-existing code in scope — conventions retroactively expose debt that was invisible without the standard.

Commit dirty file before dispatching a worker that will touch it — if a file has unrelated uncommitted changes and a worker is about to modify it, commit the dirty changes first as a standalone chore. Wave 4: CoordinateSync.ts had a captureUpdate literal fix (c984f01) committed as a chore BEFORE T17 worker dispatched, preventing worker diff confusion. Generalizable: worker-diff ambiguity between 'what I changed' and 'what was already dirty' causes review errors and makes rollback harder.

buildGeoAnchorHandler factory pattern for React-free hook testability — useGeoAnchor.ts exports a factory (buildGeoAnchorHandler) that constructs the handler object without React renderer context, mirroring useAtlasdrawTool's buildToolContext pattern. Enables unit tests that instantiate handlers directly without mounting a component. Convention: any atlas-app hook that encapsulates complex per-event logic should extract that logic into a named factory function testable without React renderer.

name: Vitest unmockable categories requiring browser smoke
description: Categories vitest+jsdom cannot catch, requiring manual browser smoke or Playwright: (1) capture-phase event ordering — fireEvent.drop fires on bubble path, never exercises capture-phase addEventListener; (2) tainted-canvas CORS errors — jsdom has no real compositor, exportPNG against real basemap tiles fails silently in tests; (3) Excalidraw Sidebar appState handshake — openSidebar state is internal to Excalidraw, jsdom cannot simulate the full appState round-trip; (4) multi-layer geo rendering — MapLibre addLayer/addSource requires real WebGL context. Pre-ship smoke checklist item: does this feature rely on any of these 4 categories? If yes, vitest alone is insufficient. Source: d121188 lesson (drop+convert) + Wave 4b/4c pre-spike doc.

content: Name functions by their return type, not their input type — inferGeometryType (MapEditor.tsx:53) names by input (FeatureCollection geometry) but returns MapLibre layer-type strings ('fill'|'line'|'circle'). This misled the T24 plan which described it as returning 'geometry kinds'. Rule: when a function transforms type vocabulary (GeoJSON geometry → MapLibre layer type), the name must reflect what it returns, not what it reads. Correct name: inferLayerType or compileLayerType. Wave 4 scrub flagged this as semantic drift requiring deliberate brief framing.

Grep lockfile before assuming yarn add is needed — OQ-W4-2 resolution: @playwright/test was already in code/apps/atlas-app/package.json (resolved 1.59.1 in lockfile). Assuming a dep is absent and dispatching a yarn add wave-cut is a false positive. Rule: before creating a dep-install wave-cut for a package, run 'grep <package> code/apps/atlas-app/package.json code/package.json' first. If already present, the wave-cut is eliminated and parallel dispatch is safe. Two wave-cut risks avoided: lockfile mutex race AND unnecessary serialization.

title: retro: atlasdraw-phase2-wave4a-tools — conventions skill landing exposes pre-existing slot debt immediately
rationale:
prediction_vs_reality: Expected Wave 4a to be a clean mechanical dispatch (T17 pure wiring, T18 factory extension) with no structural surprises; reality was that mid-session auto-bootstrap of the atlasdraw-ui-conventions skill triggered an immediate Rule-0 audit that found 3 violations in pre-existing code, adding a T29/T30/T31 retrofit track to Wave 4c — the plan didn't account for conventions skill creation as a scope-expanding event.
key_decision_reversal: The decision to defer Pin-button-to-renderTopLeftUI migration (T29) and Convert-action-to-registerAction migration (T30) to Wave 4c would be reversed if either migration proves blocked by Excalidraw internal API changes in a v0.18 patch — reversal condition: grep renderTopLeftUI or registerAction in types.ts returns a breaking signature change, at which point the slot migration must be redesigned before Wave 4c ships.
feed_forward: When a conventions skill is bootstrapped mid-session, immediately run a slot audit against all pre-existing code in scope before closing the session — the audit is cheap and the debt it surfaces is already accrued; deferring the audit into the next session risks the violations being treated as 'design' rather than 'debt' by the next worker.
trajectory: Session opened with a chore commit (c984f01) aligning CaptureUpdateAction literal before T17 dispatch; T17 shipped scaleMode 9-cell matrix wiring into _projectElement; T18 extended useGeoAnchor factory pattern to all native tools; state-close commit (609896f) bootstrapped atlasdraw-ui-conventions skill and triggered slot audit; Wave 4b/4c pre-spike doc (c194726) captured all 3 Rule-0 violations as T29/T30/T31 with verified slot APIs — full pipeline from tool wiring to UI conventions debt identification in one session.
assumption_check: The assumption that Wave 4a-tools was 'pure implementation with no design decisions' should be re-examined — T17/T18 established the buildGeoAnchorHandler factory pattern as a conventions-worthy artifact (React-free hook testability), and skill auto-bootstrap proved that session closure has scope side effects; future waves should budget for conventions audit time at close even when the wave is nominally mechanical.
